summary: A polygon's interior angle sum depends only on its number of sides, and a regular polygon splits that sum evenly.
explanation: For any polygon with \(n\) sides, the interior angles always sum to \((n-2)\times180^\circ\) — this comes from splitting the polygon into \(n-2\) triangles. In a REGULAR polygon (all sides and angles equal), divide that sum by \(n\) to get each interior angle. The exterior angles of any convex polygon always sum to \(360^\circ\), regardless of how many sides it has — a useful fact independent of the interior angle formula. A regular polygon's interior and exterior angles at each vertex are supplementary (they sum to \(180^\circ\), since they form a straight line), so once you know one you immediately know the other.
